title: Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

infobox.value: (abbreviated as **DWRF**) is a [literary festival](/wiki/Literary_festival "Literary festival") held in [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin"), in the South Island of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). Since its inception in 2014, there have been in total six festivals, including a special Celtic Noir event in 2019. The event is based mainly at the [Dunedin Public Art Gallery](/wiki/Dunedin_Public_Art_Gallery "Dunedin Public Art Gallery") but utilises [Toitū Otago Settlers Museum](/wiki/Toit%C5%AB_Otago_Settlers_Museum "Toitū Otago Settlers Museum"), the [Otago Pioneer Women's Memorial Hall](/wiki/Otago_Pioneer_Women%27s_Memorial_Hall "Otago Pioneer Women's Memorial Hall"), the [Dunedin Central Library](/wiki/Dunedin_Public_Libraries "Dunedin Public Libraries") and in 2021 the central live music venue Dog with Two Tails. Festival events include talks, book launches, workshops, a storytrain or storybus, and in some years the unveiling of a new plaque on the [Dunedin Writers' Walk](/wiki/Dunedin_Writers%27_Walk "Dunedin Writers' Walk").

outgoing_links.text: closure of New Zealand's borders for COVID-19

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Literary festival in New Zealand

| Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival | |
| --- | --- |
| Kyle Mewburn, Robyn Belton and David Elliot during the 2021 festival session "Writing for Children" | |
| Genre | [literary festival](/wiki/Literary_festival "Literary festival") |
| Country | New Zealand |
| Established | 2014 |
| Patron | [Lynley Hood](/wiki/Lynley_Hood "Lynley Hood") |
| Website | <https://www.dunedinwritersfestival.co.nz/> |

The **Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival** (abbreviated as **DWRF**) is a [literary festival](/wiki/Literary_festival "Literary festival") held in [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin"), in the South Island of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). Since its inception in 2014, there have been in total six festivals, including a special Celtic Noir event in 2019. The event is based mainly at the [Dunedin Public Art Gallery](/wiki/Dunedin_Public_Art_Gallery "Dunedin Public Art Gallery") but utilises [Toitū Otago Settlers Museum](/wiki/Toit%C5%AB_Otago_Settlers_Museum "Toitū Otago Settlers Museum"), the [Otago Pioneer Women's Memorial Hall](/wiki/Otago_Pioneer_Women%27s_Memorial_Hall "Otago Pioneer Women's Memorial Hall"), the [Dunedin Central Library](/wiki/Dunedin_Public_Libraries "Dunedin Public Libraries") and in 2021 the central live music venue Dog with Two Tails. Festival events include talks, book launches, workshops, a storytrain or storybus, and in some years the unveiling of a new plaque on the [Dunedin Writers' Walk](/wiki/Dunedin_Writers%27_Walk "Dunedin Writers' Walk").

In 2020 Hannah Molloy replaced Claire Finlayson as director of the festival.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Event history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Event history")]

The inaugural festival was held in May 2014, with events following in 2015, 2017, two in 2019, and then 2021. Previous to the establishment of the festival, there was a writers' event in Dunedin called Wordstruck!. Wordstruck! began as a writers' week initiated by playwright [Roger Hall](/wiki/Roger_Hall_(playwright) "Roger Hall (playwright)") in 1989, and then ran biennially for almost 15 years; it may have been New Zealand's first writers' week.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## 2014 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2014 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

The inaugural festival ran from Friday 9 May to Sunday 11 May 2014, and had a line-up including [Alexander McCall Smith](/wiki/Alexander_McCall_Smith "Alexander McCall Smith"), [Huw Lewis-Jones](/wiki/Huw_Lewis-Jones "Huw Lewis-Jones"), [Janice Galloway](/wiki/Janice_Galloway "Janice Galloway"), [Nigel Leask](/wiki/Nigel_Leask "Nigel Leask"), [Eleanor Catton](/wiki/Eleanor_Catton "Eleanor Catton"), [Majella Cullinane](/wiki/Majella_Cullinane "Majella Cullinane"), [Kate de Goldi](/wiki/Kate_De_Goldi "Kate De Goldi") and [Julie Le Clerc](/wiki/Julie_Le_Clerc "Julie Le Clerc"). Almost every event sold out.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) The first Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival in 2014 coincided with Dunedin's bid to become a [UNESCO City of Literature](/wiki/UNESCO_City_of_literature "UNESCO City of literature").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

## 2015 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2015 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/DPAG.jpg/250px-DPAG.jpg)](/wiki/File:DPAG.jpg)

Dunedin Public Art Gallery, venue for many of the festival events

The success of Dunedin's bid to become a City of Literature was announced in November 2014.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The 2015 festival ran 5–10 May, and included a series of literary lunches, workshops on romance, intellectual property, and writing for young adults, a "speed date an author" event, a centenary lecture recital for [W.B Yeats](/wiki/W._B._Yeats "W. B. Yeats"), and a Janet Frame memorial lecture given by [Daphne Clair de Jong](/wiki/Daphne_Clair "Daphne Clair").[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8) A plaque to writer [Dan Davin](/wiki/Dan_Davin "Dan Davin") was unveiled on the Dunedin Writers' Walk. The [Fortune Theatre](/wiki/Fortune_Theatre,_Dunedin "Fortune Theatre, Dunedin") hosted a stage adaptation of *[Mrs Dalloway](/wiki/Mrs_Dalloway "Mrs Dalloway")*, performed by [Rebecca Vaughan](/w/index.php?title=Rebecca_Vaughan_(theatre)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rebecca Vaughan (theatre) (page does not exist)").[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8) Guests included [Damian Barr](/wiki/Damian_Barr "Damian Barr"), [Liam McIlvanney](/wiki/Liam_McIlvanney "Liam McIlvanney"), [Anna Smaill](/wiki/Anna_Smaill "Anna Smaill"), [Majella Cullinane](/wiki/Majella_Cullinane "Majella Cullinane"), [Nick Davies](/wiki/Nick_Davies "Nick Davies") and [Patricia Grace](/wiki/Patricia_Grace "Patricia Grace").

## 2017 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2017 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

The 2017 event ran from 9 May to 14 May. It included, according to [Grant Smithies](/w/index.php?title=Grant_Smithies&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grant Smithies (page does not exist)"), "more laureates than you could shake a stick at", with over 80 writers, illustrators, performers and other guests.[[9]](#cite_note-:3-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) International speakers included [Ian Rankin](/wiki/Ian_Rankin "Ian Rankin"), [Stella Duffy](/wiki/Stella_Duffy "Stella Duffy"), [Hannah Kent](/wiki/Hannah_Kent "Hannah Kent"), [Miranda Carter](/wiki/Miranda_Carter "Miranda Carter") and [John Lanchester](/wiki/John_Lanchester "John Lanchester"), alongside [Bill Manhire](/wiki/Bill_Manhire "Bill Manhire"), [Victor Rodger](/wiki/Victor_Rodger "Victor Rodger"), and [Catherine Chidgey](/wiki/Catherine_Chidgey "Catherine Chidgey").[[11]](#cite_note-11) British actress [Rebecca Vaughan](/w/index.php?title=Rebecca_Vaughan_(theatre)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rebecca Vaughan (theatre) (page does not exist)") performed the one-woman play *Jane Eyre: An Autobiography*, written by [Elton Townend Jones](/w/index.php?title=Elton_Townend_Jones&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elton Townend Jones (page does not exist)").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

There were 36 events, including workshops, panel sessions, poetry readings, theatrical performances, and book launches.[[9]](#cite_note-:3-9)

## 2019 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2019 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

The 2019 event ran from 9 May to 12 May and included more than 60 writers.[[13]](#cite_note-13) International guests included [John Boyne](/wiki/John_Boyne "John Boyne"); UK hip-hop artist and author [Akala](/wiki/Akala_(rapper) "Akala (rapper)"), French picture-book author-illustrators [Eric Veillé](/w/index.php?title=Eric_Veill%C3%A9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eric Veillé (page does not exist)") and [Clotilde Perrin](/w/index.php?title=Clotilde_Perrin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clotilde Perrin (page does not exist)"), novelist [Markus Zusak](/wiki/Markus_Zusak "Markus Zusak"), feminist author [Clementine Ford](/wiki/Clementine_Ford_(writer) "Clementine Ford (writer)") and Australian Children's Laureate [Morris Gleitzman](/wiki/Morris_Gleitzman "Morris Gleitzman").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## 2019 Celtic Noir Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2019 Celtic Noir Festival")]

The 10–13 October inaugural Celtic Noir Festival featured a workshop on how to plot a thriller by Liam McIlvanney, [Fiona Kidman](/wiki/Fiona_Kidman "Fiona Kidman") talking about her true crime novel *This Mortal Boy*, [Adrian McKinty](/wiki/Adrian_McKinty "Adrian McKinty"), [Liz Nugent](/wiki/Liz_Nugent "Liz Nugent") and [Vanda Symon](/wiki/Vanda_Symon "Vanda Symon"), and [Val McDermid](/wiki/Val_McDermid "Val McDermid"), who was the Visiting Professor of Scottish Studies and Crime Fiction at the University of Otago.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## 2021 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2021 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Hannah_Parry_and_Elizabeth_Knox_at_DWRF_2021.jpg/250px-Hannah_Parry_and_Elizabeth_Knox_at_DWRF_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hannah_Parry_and_Elizabeth_Knox_at_DWRF_2021.jpg)

[H. G. Parry](/wiki/H._G._Parry "H. G. Parry") and [Elizabeth Knox](/wiki/Elizabeth_Knox "Elizabeth Knox") during the 2021 festival session "Placing Fantasy Inside the Real World"

Due to the [closure of New Zealand's borders for COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_Zealand "COVID-19 pandemic in New Zealand"), the 2021 festival featured only New Zealand-based speakers, with 55 guests over 35 events and five venues, Toitū Otago Settlers Museum, Dunedin Public Art Gallery, Dunedin Central Library, [Otago Pioneer Women's Memorial Hall](/wiki/Otago_Pioneer_Women%27s_Memorial_Hall "Otago Pioneer Women's Memorial Hall"), and cafe bar Dog with Two Tails.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) The story train that had featured at previous festivals was replaced with a story bus, departing from [Dunedin Botanic Garden](/wiki/Dunedin_Botanic_Garden "Dunedin Botanic Garden"). Speakers included [Witi Ihimaera](/wiki/Witi_Ihimaera "Witi Ihimaera"), [Elizabeth Knox](/wiki/Elizabeth_Knox "Elizabeth Knox"), [Nalini Singh](/wiki/Nalini_Singh_(author) "Nalini Singh (author)"), [Vincent O'Sullivan](/wiki/Vincent_O%27Sullivan_(New_Zealand_writer) "Vincent O'Sullivan (New Zealand writer)"), and [Liz Breslin](/w/index.php?title=Liz_Breslin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Liz Breslin (page does not exist)").[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) During the festival, a new plaque on the [Dunedin Writers' Walk](/wiki/Dunedin_Writers%27_Walk "Dunedin Writers' Walk") was unveiled, honouring romance writer [Essie Summers](/wiki/Essie_Summers "Essie Summers").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## 2023 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2023 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

The theme for the 2023 event, which ran 13–15 October, was “Te Pūao - the place where the river meets the sea”.[[19]](#cite_note-:4-19) It marked fifty years of publishing by [Witi Ihimaera](/wiki/Witi_Ihimaera "Witi Ihimaera"), and 145 years since [Katherine Mansfield](/wiki/Katherine_Mansfield "Katherine Mansfield")'s birth. The festival director was Kitty Brown ([Kāi Tahu](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Tahu "Ngāi Tahu"), [Kāti Mamoe](/wiki/K%C4%81ti_M%C4%81moe "Kāti Māmoe")).[[19]](#cite_note-:4-19) The development of the programme involved two Māori curators for the first time, law professor [Jacinta Ruru](/wiki/Jacinta_Ruru "Jacinta Ruru") and history professor [Angela Wanhalla](/wiki/Angela_Wanhalla "Angela Wanhalla").[[20]](#cite_note-:5-20) The opening event was a [pōwhiri](/wiki/P%C5%8Dwhiri "Pōwhiri") at [Ōtākou Marae](/wiki/%C5%8Ct%C4%81kou_Marae "Ōtākou Marae"), followed by a conversation between Ihimaera, [Linda Tuhiwai Smith](/wiki/Linda_Tuhiwai_Smith "Linda Tuhiwai Smith") and [Monty Soutar](/wiki/Monty_Soutar "Monty Soutar").[[21]](#cite_note-:6-21) Other events included a 'soapbox poetry' event on election day, themed around politics.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Featured writers and speakers included [Coco Solid](/wiki/Coco_Solid "Coco Solid"), [Chris Tse](/wiki/Chris_Tse_(New_Zealand_writer) "Chris Tse (New Zealand writer)"), [Emma Espiner](/wiki/Emma_Espiner "Emma Espiner"), [David Eggleton](/wiki/David_Eggleton "David Eggleton"), [Emily Writes](/wiki/Emily_Writes "Emily Writes"), [Fiona Farrell](/wiki/Fiona_Farrell "Fiona Farrell"), [Leonie Pihama](/wiki/Leonie_Pihama "Leonie Pihama"), and [Ihumātao](/wiki/Ihum%C4%81tao "Ihumātao") activist [Qiane Matata-Sipu](/wiki/Qiane_Matata-Sipu "Qiane Matata-Sipu"), speaking alongside researcher [Karen Nairn](/wiki/Karen_Nairn "Karen Nairn").[[21]](#cite_note-:6-21)[[20]](#cite_note-:5-20)

## 2025 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2025 Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival")]

No festival was held in 2024. The theme for the 2025 festival, which ran from 16 to 18 October, was "Ahi ka – keeping the home fires burning".[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The opening event and pōwhiri were held at the [Ōtākou Marae](/wiki/%C5%8Ct%C4%81kou_Marae "Ōtākou Marae"), and the festival included a fashion history talk at [Toitū Otago Settlers Museum](/wiki/Toit%C5%AB_Otago_Settlers_Museum "Toitū Otago Settlers Museum"), focused on [Eden Hore](/wiki/Eden_Hore "Eden Hore"), a poetry evening at WOOF! bar, and panels and talks by [Tāme Iti](/wiki/T%C4%81me_Iti "Tāme Iti"), [Victor Rodger](/wiki/Victor_Rodger "Victor Rodger"), [Talia Marshall](/wiki/Talia_Marshall "Talia Marshall"), [Becky Manawatu](/wiki/Becky_Manawatu "Becky Manawatu"), [Louise Wallace](/wiki/Louise_Wallace_(writer) "Louise Wallace (writer)"), [Susie Ferguson](/wiki/Susie_Ferguson "Susie Ferguson") and [Laurence Fearnley](/wiki/Laurence_Fearnley "Laurence Fearnley").[[25]](#cite_note-:7-25) [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen")'s 250th birthday was marked with a Regency costume high tea at [Tūhura Otago Museum](/wiki/T%C5%ABhura_Otago_Museum "Tūhura Otago Museum"), and the 250th issue of the literary magazine *[Landfall](/wiki/Landfall_(journal) "Landfall (journal)")* was marked with a symposium at the museum and an exhibition in Special Collections at the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago") central library.[[25]](#cite_note-:7-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)
